area: Plane Kanban/board layout: grouping, sub-grouping, swimlanes, column config, the 5 layouts, WIP, ordering, and card display properties (self-hosted Community edition)
summary: Self-hosted Community Plane (the free, no-license-key, AGPLv3 edition) has feature parity with Cloud's Free tier, which means the entire board/layout toolkit relevant to this task is available: all 5 layouts (List, Board/Kanban, Calendar, Table/spreadsheet, Timeline/Gantt), Group by, Sub-group by (the practical equivalent of swimlanes), Order by, Show empty groups, Show sub-work items, and the full set of card Display properties. Board columns are NOT independently configured — they are a pure projection of whatever you Group by. Grouping by State yields workflow columns (Backlog/Todo/In Progress/Done/Cancelled), and that column ORDER is governed by the state order in Project Settings > States, so the board is configured indirectly through states, display options, and views rather than a dedicated board-column editor. Two real constraints exist for the goal: (1) Plane has NO native WIP limits anywhere — there is no per-column card cap, so WIP must be enforced by convention/automation, not configuration; (2) private (per-user) views and estimate systems beyond 6 values require Pro/Commercial, but public per-project views, custom states, and 6-value estimates are all free. The path to a Linear-crisp / Notion-calm board is therefore: lock a clean state taxonomy, save opinionated per-layout Views with deliberately minimal Display properties, and impose WIP discipline through state-group naming + (optionally) the REST API rather than expecting UI WIP caps. Theme/legibility itself is a CSS-layer concern outside Plane's config surface — Community lets you toggle a light theme in profile settings, but crisp/dense restyling is a self-hosted frontend customization, not a setting.
key_findings: FIVE LAYOUTS, all in Community: List (grouped text rows, drag-and-drop reorder, shows parent-child), Board (Kanban cards in vertical columns, drag cards between columns, supports sub-grouping), Calendar (items mapped by due/end date, drag to change due date — only shows items WITH a due date), Table (spreadsheet grid, keyboard nav, bulk multi-row edit, columns = properties), Timeline/Gantt (start/end-date bars, dependency links). Switched via layout icons in the work-items header. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/layouts.

BOARD COLUMNS = GROUP BY PROJECTION. There is no separate board-column editor. Columns are generated by whatever 'Group by' is set in the Display dropdown. Group by State -> one column per workflow state; Group by Priority -> Urgent/High/Medium/Low/None columns; Group by Assignee/Cycle/Module/Labels/Created By/Work Item Types likewise. To change board columns you change Group by or change the underlying states/labels — not a board config panel.

GROUP BY values (Display dropdown): State, Priority, Cycle, Module, Labels, Assignees, Created By, Milestones, Releases, Work Item Types, None. SUB-GROUP BY offers the same value set and is Plane's swimlane mechanism — e.g. Group by State (columns) + Sub-group by Assignee (horizontal swimlanes) gives a 2-D Kanban. 'Swimlane' is not a named feature; sub-grouping IS the swimlane. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options.

ORDER BY (card ordering within a column/group): Manual, Last Created, Last Updated, Start Date, Due Date, Priority. 'Manual' enables true drag-and-drop hand-ordering of cards; the others are computed sorts. This is the only ordering control — there is no secondary/tie-break sort.

CARD DISPLAY PROPERTIES (toggle on/off per view): ID, Work Item Type, Assignee, Start Date, Due Date, Labels, Priority, State, Sub-work item Count, Attachment Count, Link, Estimate, Modules, Cycle. Plus two structural toggles: 'Show empty groups' (render headers for empty columns) and 'Show sub-work items' (expand children inline). All free.

STATES drive the workflow and the board column order. Five state GROUPS with default states: Backlog->'Backlog', Unstarted->'Todo', Started->'In Progress', Completed->'Done', Cancelled->'Cancelled'. Custom states are added/renamed/reordered at Project > Settings > States (+ to add to a group, pencil to edit, x to delete when unused). Reordering states reorders the board's state columns. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/states.

PRIORITY is a fixed 5-level enum: Urgent, High, Medium, Low, None (highest->lowest). Not customizable. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/properties. Work item properties available: Title (only mandatory field), Description, State, Priority, Assignees (no guests), Labels, Start date, Due date, Cycle, Modules, Parent, plus relations (Blocking / Blocked by / Relates to / Duplicate of) and external Links.

ESTIMATES exist and are free (with a cap): configured at Project Settings > Estimates > Add estimate system, then 'Enable estimates for my project'. Supports Points (Linear/Fibonacci/Squares/custom numeric), Categories (T-shirt sizes, Easy/Medium/Hard, custom), or Time (hours). Free plan / Community is limited to 6 custom estimate values per system; removing that cap needs Pro. Estimate is a toggleable card display property. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/estimates.

NO WIP LIMITS anywhere in Plane. No documented per-column card cap, no column-level WIP setting, no warning when a state/column exceeds a threshold. WIP must be handled by convention, by analytics review, or via the REST API/webhooks (both free in Community) — it is not a board configuration option.

VIEWS persist the full board configuration and are the unit of 'board design'. A View saves: layout + Group by + Sub-group by + Order by + filters + display properties. Create via Project > Views (sidebar) > Add view (name, access level, layout, display options, filters) > Create View; or configure the work-items screen and click 'Save View'; edit and 'Update View'. Public per-project views are FREE; PRIVATE (per-creator) views require Pro. Built-in workspace views (All Issues / Assigned to Me / Created by Me / Subscribed) are spreadsheet-only and non-deletable. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views.

FILTERS use Plane Query Language (PQL) and cover state, priority, assignee, label, cycle, module, created by, and date properties; filters are saved as part of a View. Filters + Display are separate dropdowns in the work-items header. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/overview.

EDITION BOUNDARY for the goal: self-hosted Community = Cloud Free parity. IN (free): all 5 layouts, grouping, sub-grouping/swimlanes, manual ordering, custom states, 6-value estimates, all card display properties, public per-project views, REST API, webhooks, intake, dashboards. OUT (needs Commercial/Pro license key): private views, estimate systems beyond 6 values, and other Pro/Business/Enterprise extras. Sources: developers.plane.so/self-hosting/editions-and-versions, self-hosting-101.

recommendations.title: Design the board through a tight State taxonomy, not a column editor
recommendations.detail: Because board columns are a projection of Group by State, the column set IS the state set. For a crisp Linear-style SDLC pipeline, define states at Project > Settings > States in pipeline order, one per group, e.g. Backlog (Backlog) -> Triage/Todo (Unstarted) -> In Progress + In Review (Started, two states) -> Done (Completed) -> Cancelled. Keep the count to 5-7 columns max for legibility; reorder them in the States UI so the board reads left-to-right as the real flow. Avoid redundant states — every extra state is an extra column the eye must scan.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/states (Project > Settings > States)

recommendations.title: Use Sub-group by sparingly as deliberate swimlanes
recommendations.detail: Plane's swimlanes = Sub-group by. The highest-readability default is Group by State with NO sub-group (single clean pipeline). Offer a second saved View 'By Owner' = Group by State + Sub-group by Assignee for standup/load-balancing, and optionally 'By Module' for architecture slices. Do not leave sub-grouping on by default — 2-D Kanban is dense and works against the calm/Notion feel unless the user opts in.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options (Display dropdown > Sub-group by)

recommendations.title: Ruthlessly minimize card Display properties for the Linear look
recommendations.detail: Linear cards are calm because they show little. Set the default board View to display only: ID, Priority, Assignee, Due Date, and Labels. Turn OFF State (redundant when grouped by State), Sub-work item Count, Attachment Count, Link, Modules, Cycle, Estimate, Start Date on the primary board to cut visual noise. Expose the richer fields in the Table layout instead, where columns are expected. Save these as a 'Board (clean)' View so the minimal property set is the persisted default.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options (Display dropdown > Display Properties) + https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views

recommendations.title: Set Order by = Priority (or Manual) and turn off Show empty groups for a tidy board
recommendations.detail: For machine + human readability, default the board to Order by Priority so Urgent/High float to the top of every column deterministically; switch to Manual only when the team wants hand-curated ordering (it enables drag-to-reorder). Disable 'Show empty groups' on the board so dead columns don't clutter the canvas; enable it only on planning Views where seeing an empty 'In Review' is itself signal. Keep 'Show sub-work items' off on the board to avoid nested clutter.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options (Order by; Show empty groups; Show sub-work items)

recommendations.title: Encode every board configuration as a small set of named public Views
recommendations.detail: Views are the only durable way to ship a designed board. Author a curated set per project — e.g. 'Pipeline' (Board, Group=State, Order=Priority, minimal props), 'By Owner' (Board, Sub-group=Assignee), 'This Cycle' (Board filtered to active Cycle), 'Roadmap' (Timeline), 'Triage' (Table filtered to Backlog) — and create them under Project > Views as PUBLIC (private requires Pro, which Community lacks). These become the team's shared, reproducible board states. Provision them via the REST API for consistency across projects.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views (Project > Views > Add view); https://developers.plane.so/api-reference/issue/overview

recommendations.title: Implement WIP limits as convention + automation, since Plane has none
recommendations.detail: There is no built-in WIP cap. To get observability toward the goal: (a) put soft limits in state/column names is discouraged (clutters labels) — instead (b) use Analytics/Dashboards to watch column counts, and (c) enforce caps programmatically via webhooks + REST API (both free in Community) — subscribe to work-item state-change events, count items in a 'Started'-group state, and post a flag (comment/label) when a column exceeds N. This keeps the board visually clean while still enforcing flow limits machine-side.
recommendations.where: https://developers.plane.so/dev-tools/intro-webhooks + https://developers.plane.so/api-reference/issue/overview; analytics: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/analytics

recommendations.title: Enable a 6-value estimate system and surface it only where it earns space
recommendations.detail: Estimates are free up to 6 values in Community — enough for Fibonacci 1/2/3/5/8/13 or XS-XL. Enable at Project Settings > Estimates for capacity/observability, but keep the Estimate display property OFF on the calm board and ON in the Table/spreadsheet View and Gantt, where sizing data belongs. This preserves the Linear-clean board while giving machines/dashboards the structured estimate field. Do not design for >6 values — that needs Pro.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/estimates (Project Settings > Estimates > Enable estimates)

recommendations.title: Treat the light-theme/crisp-dense restyle as a self-hosted frontend customization, not a Plane setting
recommendations.detail: Plane's config surface controls structure (states, grouping, views, properties) but NOT the crisp/dense/Notion-refined visual layer beyond the built-in light/dark theme toggle in user profile settings. To reach Linear-grade legibility (tighter card padding, calmer borders, refined type scale, light surface palette) you must customize the self-hosted web frontend's CSS/Tailwind theme tokens at the code layer — this is outside Plane's admin/config menus. Plan a CSS-theme pass against the self-hosted web app as a separate workstream from the board-config recommendations above.
recommendations.where: User profile > Theme toggle (in-app light/dark); deeper restyle = self-hosted web frontend code, see https://developers.plane.so/self-hosting/overview

area: Plane Community states, groups, ordering, workflows, automations
summary: States are fully configurable in self-hosted Community Plane; Workflows, Automations and Approvals are paywalled.
key_findings: Five fixed state groups (backlog, unstarted, started, completed, cancelled) with unlimited custom states each, managed at Project Settings -> States. (https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/states)

Ordering is per-group via an auto-generated sequence field; default state is settable. States API exposes name, color, group, sequence, default. (https://developers.plane.so/api-reference/state/overview)

Native Workflows (transition rules) require Business; Approvals require Enterprise Grid; Custom Automations require Business. Not in Community. (https://docs.plane.so/workflows-and-approvals/workflows)

Board Display dropdown (Community) offers Group by State, Show empty groups, Order by, and property toggles; Board layout = one column per state in group+sequence order. (https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options)

recommendations.title: Define an ordered SDLC state taxonomy in the five groups
recommendations.detail: Backlog/Triage, Todo/Ready/Blocked, In Progress/In Review/QA, Done/Released, Cancelled/Wont Do; set default state explicitly.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/states

recommendations.title: Enforce transitions via webhooks since native Workflows are paywalled
recommendations.detail: Register a Community webhook on work-item update and validate state changes against an allowed-transition matrix via REST.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/workflows-and-approvals/workflows

area: Plane Saved Views, filters, display options, and project Pages/wiki (self-hosted Community Edition)
summary: Self-hosted Community Edition (CE) ships at parity with Plane Cloud's Free tier and gives you everything you need for a high-legibility PM-SDLC board: unlimited Saved Views, all 5 layouts (List, Board/Kanban, Calendar, Table/Spreadsheet, Timeline/Gantt), the full filter builder, the full Display-options menu (group by, sub-group by, order by, show sub-items, show empty groups, and per-card display properties), and unlimited project Pages with a Notion-style block editor (slash commands, markdown, work-item @mentions, version history, table of contents, archive, export). The board-level legibility goal is fully achievable on CE through Views + Display options alone. The CSS/theme restyle toward Linear is a self-hosted code lever (the web app is Next.js + Tailwind, AGPL source on GitHub), not a settings toggle. Edition boundary matters: a handful of options the docs list as a single menu are Pro-only and will be absent or inert on CE — workspace Wiki (project Pages still work), custom Work Item Types + Milestones/Releases as group-by dimensions, private/locked/published Views, published/moved Pages, CSV/XLSX/JSON export, and Plane AI. Recommendations below lean entirely on CE-available levers: a small set of canonical Saved Views with locked-down display conventions, a Pages-based living wiki, and a Linear-crisp / Notion-calm light theme applied via the self-hosted web codebase. The official end-user docs live at docs.plane.so (core-concepts/views, /issues/display-options, /issues/layouts, /issues/visualise_filter, /pages/overview); self-hosting/edition docs at developers.plane.so.
key_findings: VIEW SCOPES (CE): Plane has three view scopes — Project Views (per project, support all 5 layouts, toggleable in Project Settings), Workspace Views (workspace-wide, spreadsheet layout, with four non-deletable defaults: All Issues, Assigned to Me, Created by Me, Subscribed), and Teamspace Views (Pro/teamspace feature). A Saved View stores filters + layout + display options (grouping, columns, empty-group visibility) + sorting + access level. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views.

CREATE A VIEW (exact path): Project View — open project, Views in the project sidebar, Add view, set name/description/access/layout/display options/filters, Create View. Faster path: apply filters + layout in the Work Items section, then click Save View in the filter bar. Edit by changing filters/display and clicking Update View. Workspace View — Views in the main (workspace) sidebar, Add View. Favorite via the star icon; Duplicate creates a 'Copy of' variant; Delete is permanent and does not touch work items. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views.

DISPLAY OPTIONS MENU (the legibility control center): the Display dropdown exposes — Group by {State, Priority, Cycle, Module, Labels, Assignees, Created By, None} (plus Pro-only Work Item Types, Milestones, Releases); Sub-group by {same list}; Order by {Manual, Last Created, Last Updated, Start Date, Due Date, Priority}; toggle Show sub-work items (expands parent rows to show sub-issues inline); toggle Show empty groups; and per-card Display Properties to show/hide {ID, Work item Type*, Assignee, Start Date, Due Date, Labels, Priority, State, Sub-work item Count, Attachment Count, Link, Estimate, Modules, Cycle} (* Type column is Pro custom-type aware). Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options.

FILTERS (full builder, CE): the Filters button (in the row below the main toolbar) opens a dropdown with — Title, Type*, State, State Group, Assignees, Priority, Mentions, Label, Cycle, Module, Start date, Due date, Created at, Updated at, Created by (plus Pro-only Milestone and Custom properties). Filters combine with implicit AND. Operators vary by field type: selection fields support is / is any of / is not / is not any of / is empty; date fields support is/is not/before/after/before-or-on/after-or-on/between/not between/is empty; text fields support contains/does not contain. Clear individual filters with X, Clear all to reset, Save view to persist. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/visualise_filter.

LAYOUTS (all 5 in CE): List (text-dense, group + drag-drop, parent-child hierarchy), Board/Kanban (columns by group, sub-grouping, drag between columns), Table/Spreadsheet (rows + attribute columns, keyboard nav, bulk edit), Calendar (by due date, drag to reschedule), Timeline/Gantt (bars with start/end, dependencies, critical path, drag to adjust). Layout switcher sits in the view toolbar. Group by / order by / show-sub-items apply most fully to List and Board; Table is column-driven; Calendar and Timeline are date-driven. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/layouts.

PROJECT PAGES / WIKI (CE): project Pages are a built-in Notion-style knowledge base. Create with key D in a project, or Pages in the sidebar -> Add page. Editor: slash command / for ~16 block types (lists, images, tables, work-item embeds), static formatting toolbar (H1-H6, bold/italic/underline/strikethrough, text+background color, alignment), native markdown, @ to mention/link work items, drag-handle reordering. Page ... menu (CE subset): Full width, Copy markdown, Copy page link, Make a copy, Lock Page, Archive Page, Version history (timestamped, restorable), Export Page (PDF/Word/Markdown). Real-time multi-cursor collaboration, table of contents panel, and an info panel (word/char count, read time, last editor, created date). Public vs private visibility via the ... menu. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/pages/overview.

EDITION BOUNDARY (critical for self-hosted CE): CE = Plane Cloud Free-tier parity — unlimited projects/work-items/cycles/modules/pages, all 5 layouts, intake, dashboards, estimates, full REST API + webhooks, native importers, no seat limit. PRO-ONLY (absent or inert on CE): workspace Wiki (project Pages still fully work), custom Work Item Types + Epics/Initiatives, Milestones/Releases (so those group-by/filter dimensions won't appear), private Views, Lock View, Publish View, Publish/Move Page, Export View to CSV/XLSX/JSON, time tracking, teamspaces, and Plane AI credits. Plane AI for self-hosted (v2.4.0, bring-your-own-LLM via .env) is positioned for Commercial self-hosted, not free CE. Sources: developers.plane.so/self-hosting/editions-and-versions, plane.so/self-hosted, plane.so/changelog/release-v-2-4-0-plane-ai-now-available, plane.so/pro.

THEME/RESTYLE LEVER: there is no in-product theme editor for a Linear/Notion light look beyond Plane's built-in Light/Dark toggle. Achieving 'crisp, dense, calm' Linear + refined Notion light feel is a self-hosted code change against the AGPL web app (Next.js + Tailwind, makeplane/plane on GitHub) — adjust the Tailwind theme/CSS custom properties (color tokens, spacing/density, font stack, border + shadow weights) in the web package. This is a custom-property / css-theme lever, not a workflow-config setting.

recommendations.title: Define a canonical set of Saved Project Views (the board's 'lenses')
recommendations.detail: Create a small, named, public set of Project Views so every reader sees the same legible cut of the work: (1) 'Sprint Board' — Board layout, Group by State, Order by Priority, filtered to the active Cycle; (2) 'My Work' — List, Group by State, filter Assignees = me; (3) 'Triage / Intake' — List, Group by Priority, filter State Group = Backlog; (4) 'Roadmap' — Timeline/Gantt, Order by Start Date, showing dependencies; (5) 'Audit / All' — Table layout with every display property on, Order by Last Updated for full observability. Build each via Work Items -> apply filters + layout -> Save View, or Views -> Add view. Keep them public so they are shared, machine-readable lenses rather than personal state.
recommendations.where: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views

recommendations.title: Lock down Display-option conventions for maximal legibility
recommendations.detail: Standardize the Display dropdown across the canonical views: Group by State for flow boards / Priority for triage; Order by Priority (planning) or Last Updated (observability); turn ON Show sub-work items so parent/child structure is visible inline; turn OFF Show empty groups on dense boards to reduce noise (turn ON only on the Sprint Board so an empty 'Done' column still reads as a column). For card Display Properties, enable a consistent crisp set — ID, Assignee, Priority, State, Due Date, Labels, Sub-work item Count — and hide the rest to keep cards calm and scannable (Linear-style). Use the Table/'Audit' view as the maximal-readability machine-readable cut with all properties on.
recommendations.where: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/display-options

recommendations.title: Use the filter builder for transparent, reproducible queries
recommendations.detail: Encode review-ready queries as Saved Views using the AND-combined filters: e.g. Priority is any of {Urgent, High} AND State Group is not Completed AND Updated at after [date] surfaces stale high-priority work for observability. Prefer State Group over individual States for stable, machine-readable buckets. Avoid relying on Milestone/Custom-property filters — those are Pro-only and will be missing on CE.
recommendations.where: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/visualise_filter

recommendations.title: Match each layout to its job; default the board to Board + the audit to Table
recommendations.detail: Board/Kanban for active-sprint flow (state columns), Table/Spreadsheet for the high-density machine-readable audit cut and bulk edits, Timeline/Gantt for the roadmap with dependencies, Calendar for deadline planning, List for personal/triage scanning. All five are CE-available, so assign one layout per canonical view rather than asking readers to re-switch. Pro-only Teamspace views are out of scope on CE.
recommendations.where: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/layouts

recommendations.title: Stand up a living project wiki with Pages and work-item @mentions
recommendations.detail: Use project Pages (key D, or Pages -> Add page) as the human-and-machine-readable knowledge layer: a project home page, PRD/spec pages, decision log, and runbooks. Lean on @ work-item mentions to link docs to tasks (creating a navigable doc<->task graph), the table-of-contents panel for structure, version history for an audit trail, and slash-command embeds for tables/work-item blocks. Note for CE: workspace Wiki, Publish, and Move Page are Pro — keep the knowledge base inside project Pages and share via Copy page link / Export Page (PDF/Word/Markdown).
recommendations.where: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/pages/overview

recommendations.title: Apply a Linear-crisp / Notion-calm light theme via the self-hosted web codebase
recommendations.detail: Beyond the built-in Light/Dark toggle there is no in-product themer, so restyle in the AGPL web app (makeplane/plane, Next.js + Tailwind). Target the Tailwind color tokens / CSS custom properties: a clean near-white background, low-saturation neutral grays, a single calm accent, tighter row heights and reduced card padding for Linear-style density, a refined system/Inter font stack, and lighter 1px borders with minimal shadows for the Notion feel. This is a code-level custom-property/CSS lever applied at build/deploy of your self-hosted instance, not a settings change — keep it in a themed override layer so it survives version upgrades.
recommendations.where: developers.plane.so/self-hosting/editions-and-versions

summary: Community Plane covers settings, roles, toggles, identifiers; private views and custom roles are paid; theming is a frontend build.
key_findings: Workspace and project roles are independent; feature toggles, identifiers, and States are all configurable per project; theming is not a setting.

recommendations.title: Lock config levers, theme in frontend
recommendations.detail: Short uppercase identifiers, one State set, uniform toggles, Public access, scoped roles, one Intake door, harden God Mode; build the light theme in the web frontend.
recommendations.where: docs.plane.so projects overview

area: Appearance/theme options, white-label/branding/custom-CSS, configurable-vs-hard-coded in self-hosted community Plane
summary: Self-hosted Community Plane (AGPL, makeplane/plane) ships a per-user theme system with five presets — System, Light, Dark, High contrast, and Custom — plus a Custom theme that the current code accepts as a 2-color input (brand/primary + neutral/background) and expands, via an OKLCH engine, into full 14-shade palettes mapped to CSS variables. Theme is per-user and follows the account across all workspaces and devices; it is NOT a per-workspace or instance-admin setting. There is NO white-labeling, NO logo replacement, NO custom-CSS injection, and NO instance-admin branding in the Community edition — those are absent everywhere in the codebase and docs, and the only instance-level visual setting (God Mode) is an Unsplash key for project cover images. Because the entire UI is driven by semantic CSS tokens (--neutral-*, --brand-*, --color-layer-*, border-subtle, text-custom-*) compiled into Tailwind, the realistic path to a Linear/Notion light-theme restyle is (a) a Custom theme set to a near-white neutral + restrained accent for runtime config without forking, and for a deeper restyle (b) editing the token source / Tailwind theme in the self-hosted build — the colors are NOT individually hard-coded, they resolve from these variables, which is what makes a global retheme tractable. NOTE a docs-vs-code drift: docs.plane.so still describes the legacy 5-color custom theme (Background, Text, Primary, Sidebar bg, Sidebar text); the live code (theme-legacy.ts marked @deprecated) has migrated to the 2-color brand+neutral OKLCH system, so the in-app form a current self-hosted build shows may differ from the docs.
key_findings: THEME PRESETS (per-user): Profile photo (sidebar) -> Settings -> Preferences -> Theme dropdown offers System, Light, Dark, High contrast, and Custom. Default is Light. Source: docs.plane.so/core-concepts/account/settings.

CUSTOM THEME, as documented (legacy): selecting Custom exposes 5 hex inputs — Background, Text, Primary/accent, Sidebar background, Sidebar text — then a 'Set theme' button applies them. This is the docs-site description.

CUSTOM THEME, as implemented (current code): apps/web/core/components/core/theme/custom-theme-selector.tsx and ce/components/preferences/theme-switcher.tsx call applyCustomTheme(primary, background, mode) — the live form takes only 2 colors (primary/brand + background/neutral) plus a darkPalette toggle. packages/utils/src/theme-legacy.ts is marked @deprecated; migrateLegacyTheme() converts the old 5-color theme to the new 2-color system.

OKLCH ENGINE: packages/utils/src/theme/theme-application.ts generateThemePalettes() expands the 2 input colors into 14-shade palettes (SHADE_STOPS 50..1000) in OKLCH space, auto-inverts for dark mode, computes WCAG-contrast on-color text, and writes CSS variables --neutral-{shade}, --brand-{shade}, --alpha-white/black-{shade}, --txt-on-color, --editor-colors-*. This is the accessibility-aware core of theming.

TOKEN ARCHITECTURE (key for restyle): the UI is built on semantic tokens, not literal colors — e.g. bg-layer-1, border-subtle, text-custom-background-100 (seen in packages/ui/src/badge/helper.tsx). Components reference variables that resolve from the generated palettes, so a global retheme is a token change, not a per-component edit. Colors are NOT hard-coded per component.
SCOPE: 'Your selected theme applies across all workspaces and devices where you're logged in' — theme is per-user/account, NOT per-workspace and NOT an instance-admin control.
OTHER PREFERENCES in the same Preferences pane: first day of week, smooth cursor animation, timezone, and language (19 languages). These are per-user UI prefs, not branding.

NO WHITE-LABEL / NO CUSTOM CSS / NO LOGO REPLACEMENT in Community: repo searches for whitelabel, INSTANCE_LOGO, and custom-CSS injection return nothing applicable; God Mode instance-admin only exposes an Unsplash API key (Images in Plane) — no theme, logo, or color controls. Source: developers.plane.so instance-admin / govern docs.

EDITIONS: Community Edition (AGPL v3.0) is at feature parity with Cloud Free; Commercial/Airgapped are closed-source for enterprise. Docs do not gate any *theming/branding* feature behind paid tiers — the theming that exists is in Community; white-labeling simply does not exist as a documented feature in any edition's public docs.

recommendations.title: Use the built-in Custom theme as the zero-fork light baseline
recommendations.detail: In each user's Profile -> Settings -> Preferences -> Theme -> Custom, set a near-white neutral/background (e.g. #FFFFFF or a hair off-white like #FBFBFA for the Notion-paper feel) and a restrained, single accent as primary/brand (a calm indigo/blue around #5B5BD6–#3F76FF reads Linear-crisp without shouting). The OKLCH engine auto-derives the full 14-shade neutral ramp, so you get consistent borders/surfaces/text contrast for free. This is the only runtime, no-rebuild lever and it ships in Community. Caveat: it's per-user, so for a shared board every member must set it (or you script it via the account API).
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/account/settings

recommendations.title: For a true house light theme, retheme at the token layer in your self-hosted build
recommendations.detail: Because the UI resolves from semantic tokens (--neutral-*, --brand-*, --color-layer-*, border-subtle, text-custom-*) rather than literal hex, a Linear/Notion restyle is a token edit, not a per-screen rewrite. In the makeplane/plane source you self-host, adjust the neutral ramp toward cool, low-chroma greys (Linear's calm), tighten the accent to one brand hue, and lean on the existing high-contrast preset's logic for legibility. Keep edits in the OKLCH palette inputs / Tailwind theme config so dark mode + WCAG on-color text keep working. This is the maximal-legibility, observability-friendly path and stays within AGPL as long as you honor the license.
recommendations.where: https://github.com/makeplane/plane/tree/preview/packages/utils/src/theme

recommendations.title: Get density and calm via spacing/typography tokens, not color
recommendations.detail: Linear's 'dense but calm' comes mostly from tight, consistent spacing, smaller type steps, and quiet borders — not color. Target the spacing scale, row paddings (packages/ui/src/row), and border-subtle/divider tokens rather than re-coloring. Prefer hairline border-subtle over heavy separators, reduce card/list vertical padding one step, and keep a single accent reserved for active/selected states only. This raises machine + human readability (predictable rhythm) without fighting the theme engine.
recommendations.where: https://github.com/makeplane/plane/tree/preview/packages/ui/src

recommendations.title: Standardize board readability via Views/display config, not styling
recommendations.detail: For the 'maximal observability' PM-SDLC goal, much of the legibility win is configuration, not CSS: save Spreadsheet/List/Kanban Views with explicit Group-by (state/assignee/cycle), Order-by, and a curated visible-property set so every status, owner, estimate, and date is on-screen and machine-parseable. Pin these as default project Views so the board reads consistently for humans and for API/agent consumers. This pairs with the light theme to deliver transparency without custom code.
recommendations.where: https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/views/views-overview

recommendations.title: Do NOT plan around white-label/logo/custom-CSS in Community — it isn't there
recommendations.detail: There is no instance-admin branding, no logo replacement, and no custom-CSS injection in Community Plane (God Mode only exposes an Unsplash key for cover images). If brand-mark replacement is a hard requirement, scope it as either a paid Commercial/Enterprise conversation with Plane or an explicit source modification in your self-hosted fork (asset + favicon + login-page swap), and budget it as engineering work, not configuration. Don't promise it as a toggle.
recommendations.where: https://developers.plane.so/self-hosting/editions-and-versions

area: Linear board/cycle UX patterns — density, keyboard-first, calm visual language, status pills, grouping, card design — synthesized into a concrete light-theme restyle spec for a Plane-rendered PM-SDLC board (Linear-crisp + Notion-refined)
summary: Linear "feels fast" not because of animation but because of restraint applied systematically: a near-monochrome surface ramp where color is reserved almost entirely for status/priority semantics, a perceptually-uniform color space (LCH/OKLCH) so every status hue reads at equal weight, high text-on-surface contrast, tight but consistent 4px-grid spacing, iconographic status (filled-arc SVGs, not just colored dots), and a keyboard-first interaction model where every property is inline-editable. The legibility win for a PM-SDLC control plane is that the SAME discipline serves machine readability: one semantic token per state, status encoded as both icon-shape AND color (never color alone), and a stable grid that a scraper or a screenshot-diff can rely on. The restyle target is a clean light theme — white/near-white canvas (oklch ~0.99), a 5-step neutral surface ramp, one indigo accent used sparingly, and a fixed status palette tuned to equal lightness — delivered against Plane's Tailwind + CSS-custom-property theming so it is an override layer, not a fork. Plane already exposes board grouping/sub-grouping, display-property toggles, and CSS variables (--color-background-*, --color-text-*, --color-border-*, --color-primary-*) that map almost 1:1 onto Linear's surface/text/border/accent alias model, so most recommendations are css-theme + plane-setting levers rather than code rewrites. Below: concrete values (px, hex, oklch, contrast ratios) tagged by lever. Note: exact private Linear token values are not publicly published; the neutral ramp hexes are from a live-DOM extraction (Copycats) and the color-space/contrast philosophy is from Linear's own redesign writeup — I reconstruct equal-lightness status hues to Linear's stated method rather than claiming their literal values.
key_findings: WHY LINEAR FEELS FAST = RESTRAINT, NOT MOTION. Linear's own redesign writeup states the goal was a 'more neutral and timeless appearance' achieved by LIMITING how much chrome (their blue accent) appears, and by raising contrast: 'text and neutral icons darker in light mode.' Speed is a perceptual artifact of (a) low chromatic noise so the eye isn't taxed, (b) high text contrast so nothing is squinted at, and (c) a rigid grid so layout never reflows surprisingly. The board has near-zero gratuitous animation. Takeaway for the PM-SDLC board: spend your color budget on SEMANTICS (status/priority), keep everything else grayscale.

PERCEPTUALLY-UNIFORM COLOR SPACE IS THE KEYSTONE. Linear moved off HSL to LCH specifically because LCH/OKLCH is perceptually uniform: 'a red and a yellow with lightness 50 appear roughly equally light.' This is the single most important transferable idea for a status board: define every status/priority hue at a FIXED L (lightness) and only vary H (hue), so 'Backlog gray', 'In Progress yellow', 'In Review blue', 'Blocked red', 'Done green' all carry identical visual weight and none screams louder than the others. In HSL, yellow at the same numeric lightness as blue looks far brighter — that asymmetry is exactly what makes most kanban boards feel loud/cheap.

LINEAR'S THEME SURFACE IS A 3-VARIABLE SYSTEM, NOT A 98-COLOR PALETTE. They collapsed ~98 per-theme definitions down to THREE inputs: base color, accent color, contrast. From those, opacities of black/white generate aliases for surfaces, text, icons, controls across elevation tiers (background → foreground → panel → dialog → modal). Practical consequence: do NOT hand-pick 40 grays. Pick one base + one accent + a contrast level and derive the ramp by layering black/white alpha. This is also what makes themes swappable and machine-predictable.

EXACT NEUTRAL RAMP (from live-DOM extraction of linear.app). Dark-on-light anchors observed: near-black text #08090A, dark gray #62666D, mid gray #8A8F98 (the canonical 'secondary text' / muted gray), light border/divider #E5E5E6, off-white surface #F7F8F8, plus periwinkle-indigo accent ~#8FA6FF (dark-mode-leaning; in light mode the accent reads as a deeper indigo). Type: Inter Variable for body, Inter Display for headings, Berkeley Mono for code/IDs. Heading scale observed 18/20/24/32/40/64. These are the concrete grayscale targets to translate into a light theme.

STATUS IS ICON-SHAPE + COLOR, NEVER COLOR ALONE. Linear's status indicator is a small (~14px) SVG with distinct GEOMETRY per state: empty dashed circle (Backlog), solid empty circle (Todo), partial pie/arc fill that grows with progress (In Progress / In Review), full checkmark-disc (Done), X-disc or solid (Canceled). Priority is a separate glyph (signal bars / orange-filled triangle for Urgent). This dual-encoding is THE accessibility + machine-readability lever: a colorblind human and an OCR/vision model both read state from shape, with color as reinforcement, not the sole channel. Contrast-ratio of the status color is therefore allowed to stay low/calm because it's not load-bearing alone.

PILLS ARE QUIET, NOT CANDY. Linear's property chips (labels, status text, project) are low-saturation: a faint tinted background (the status hue at ~8-12% alpha over the surface) with the SAME hue at full strength only for the 8-12px dot/icon and the text. No hard borders, no drop shadows, ~4-6px radius, 11-12px text, generous horizontal padding (~6-8px) but tight vertical (~2-3px). Labels often render as just a colored 6px dot + plain text — the dot carries the color, the text stays neutral #08090A/#62666D. This is the opposite of bold filled badges and is why the board reads calm.

CARD/ROW DENSITY: TIGHT, FIXED, SCANNABLE. Linear's list rows are a single line ~36-40px tall: [status icon][priority glyph][ID mono e.g. ENG-123 in muted gray][title in near-black][spacer][labels as dots][assignee avatar 16-20px]. Board cards are slightly taller multiline but still terse — Linear explicitly does NOT render descriptions on cards and drops overflow properties rather than wrap. The discipline 'truncate, never wrap; drop, never crowd' is what keeps every card the same height and the column scannable. Avatar 16-20px, single line title with ellipsis, max ~2-3 visible property chips.

KEYBOARD-FIRST = INLINE EVERYTHING. Every property (status, assignee, priority, label, estimate, cycle) is click-to-edit IN PLACE with no modal/tab switch; Cmd/Ctrl+B toggles board/list; a command menu (Cmd+K) and single-key shortcuts drive navigation. For a control plane this matters because it makes the board operable at the same speed an agent operates the API — human and machine act on the same atomic, addressable fields. The design implication: each property must be an independently-targetable, labeled hit-target (good for humans, good for automation/testing/observability).

GROUPING + SUB-GROUPING + STICKY HEADERS = THE OBSERVABILITY GRID. Linear groups by status/assignee/project/priority/cycle/label/parent/team and sub-groups into swimlanes; group headers stay STICKY on scroll and show a count badge; empty groups are toggleable. For a PM-SDLC board this is the native way to expose the 12-state agent workflow as columns and overlay a second axis (e.g. swimlane by epic or by actor/agent). The count badge per group is a free, high-value observability signal (WIP per state). Plane supports board grouping and sub-grouping, so this is a configuration lever, not a build.

NOTION-REFINED LAYER = SOFTER SURFACES + WARMER NEUTRALS + ROOMIER RADII. Where Linear is crisp/cool, Notion's refinement comes from: pure-white #FFFFFF canvas, near-black #000000/#191919 text, a warm-gray hover/surface (#F6F5F4 / rgba(0,0,0,0.03-0.06) on hover), a 4px spacing scale (4/8/12/16/24/32/48/64), slightly larger radii (8-12px on containers vs Linear's 4-6px), and hover affordances that are barely-there gray washes rather than borders. Blending the two: take Linear's monochrome-discipline + equal-lightness status hues, and Notion's white canvas + 6-8px radii + soft gray hover wash. Result = crisp+dense but warm and calm.

CONTRAST TARGETS ARE EXPLICIT AND MUST BE HIT. Linear raised text contrast deliberately for the light theme. Concrete WCAG targets for the restyle: primary text on canvas ≥ 7:1 (AAA body) — #08090A on #FFFFFF is ~20:1; secondary/muted text ≥ 4.5:1 — #62666D on white is ~5.6:1 (passes AA), #8A8F98 on white is ~3.4:1 so reserve it for tertiary/decorative ONLY, not body; status-pill TEXT must hit 4.5:1 against the pill's tinted background (this is why the text stays near-black, not the status hue); borders/dividers can sit at ~1.3-1.5:1 (#E5E5E6 on white) since they're non-text. UI control/focus ring (the indigo accent) should hit 3:1 against adjacent surfaces.

PLANE ALREADY EXPOSES THE RIGHT KNOBS. Plane's frontend is Tailwind + a shared @plane/tailwind-config with CSS custom properties (--color-background-100/90/80, --color-text-100/200/300/400, --color-border-100/200/300, --color-primary-*). These map almost 1:1 onto Linear's surface/text/border/accent alias model, so the restyle is an OVERRIDE of those variables (a css-theme lever) plus Plane's built-in display-property and grouping settings (plane-setting/workflow-config levers) — NOT a fork of the component tree. Status/state colors in Plane are configured per-state in workspace settings (workflow-config lever), which is where the equal-lightness hue palette gets applied.

recommendations.title: Adopt a 5-step monochrome light surface ramp; reserve color for semantics only
recommendations.detail: Override Plane's --color-background-* / --color-text-* / --color-border-* with a Linear-derived light ramp. Concrete values: canvas/page = #FFFFFF (oklch 1 0 0); raised surface / sidebar / column bg = #FBFBFA (oklch ~0.991 0.001 95, a hair warm, Notion-leaning); card/row hover wash = #F6F5F4 (oklch ~0.976 0.001 95) or rgba(0,0,0,0.035); subtle panel = #F1F1EF; border/divider = #E8E8E6 / #E5E5E6 (oklch ~0.917 0.001 95); primary text = #08090A (oklch ~0.16 0.003 265, ~20:1 on white); secondary text = #5C5F66 / #62666D (~5.6:1, AA); tertiary/decorative only = #8A8F98 (~3.4:1 — never body). RULE: the canvas, cards, sidebar, headers are ALL within this 5-step gray ramp. Color appears ONLY on status icons, priority glyphs, label dots, and the single accent. This monochrome-everything-except-semantics split is the #1 driver of the 'calm, fast, legible' feel.
recommendations.where: Plane web theme CSS custom properties (override --color-background-100/90/80/70, --color-text-100/200/300, --color-border-100/200/300 in the injected theme stylesheet / @plane/tailwind-config consumer)

recommendations.title: Define status + priority colors at EQUAL OKLCH lightness (perceptual uniformity)
recommendations.detail: Set every workflow-state color and priority color at a FIXED lightness L and vary only hue H, so no state visually outweighs another. Recommended light-theme set at L≈0.62, C≈0.13: Backlog/neutral = #8A8F98 (gray, C≈0); Todo/Unstarted = oklch(0.62 0.04 265) muted slate; In Progress = oklch(0.74 0.14 85) amber/yellow #E0A33A-ish (raise L slightly only for yellow per OKLCH if it reads heavy); In Review = oklch(0.62 0.13 250) blue #4C6EF5-cousin; Blocked/Urgent = oklch(0.58 0.17 27) red #E5484D; Done = oklch(0.62 0.13 150) green #2F9E44-cousin; Canceled = #8A8F98 gray. The point is not the exact hex but that all status hues share L so the column reads balanced. Apply these as the per-state colors in Plane workspace state settings; the same hue powers the status SVG and the label dot.
recommendations.where: Plane workspace → States configuration (per-state color) for the 12-state agent workflow; mirror the same hue tokens as --color-status-* custom properties for pill backgrounds

recommendations.title: Dual-encode status as icon-SHAPE + color (machine + colorblind readable), never color alone
recommendations.detail: Replace bare colored dots with Linear-style ~14px status SVGs whose GEOMETRY encodes state: dashed-ring (Backlog), hollow ring (Todo), arc/pie fill proportional to progress (In Progress / In Review — fill 25/50/75%), checkmark-disc (Done), X or slash-disc (Canceled/Blocked). Priority = separate glyph: 3 ascending bars (Low/Med/High) and a filled triangle for Urgent. This guarantees state is legible to a vision model, an OCR scrape, a screenshot-diff, and a colorblind human — color becomes reinforcement, not the sole channel, which is exactly why Linear can keep the colors low-saturation and calm. Critical for an 'observability/machine-readability' mandate.
recommendations.where: Plane state-icon rendering — supply custom SVG status glyphs per state (icon slot in state config / a CSS background-image set keyed to data-state attributes on cards and rows)

recommendations.title: Restyle property pills as quiet tinted chips, not bold badges
recommendations.detail: Pill spec: background = status/label hue at 10% alpha over surface (e.g. oklch from the state hue with alpha 0.10, or a precomputed tint like #4C6EF5 @ 10% = #ECEFFE); text = near-black #08090A or the hue at full strength only if it still hits 4.5:1; NO border, NO shadow; border-radius 5px; font 11.5-12px / weight 500; padding 2px 7px; the colored 6-8px dot or status icon carries the saturation, the text stays neutral. Labels can degrade to just a 6px dot + plain #62666D text. Contrast gate: pill text must be ≥4.5:1 vs the tinted bg — verify each. This single change converts a 'candy' kanban into a calm Linear-grade board.
recommendations.where: Plane card/list property-chip components — target the label, state, and priority pill classes via the theme stylesheet (background-color, color, border-radius, padding, font-size, font-weight; strip border/box-shadow)

recommendations.title: Lock card/row to a fixed-height, truncate-never-wrap, drop-never-crowd density
recommendations.detail: List row: 36px (compact) / 40px (comfortable) fixed height, single line, vertical-center; layout [14px status][12px priority][ID in Berkeley Mono / mono fallback 12px #8A8F98][title 13px #08090A, single line text-overflow:ellipsis][flex spacer][≤3 label dots][16-20px assignee avatar]. Board card: 8-12px internal padding, title 13px max 2 lines then ellipsis, a single property row of ≤3 chips, avatar bottom-right; DO NOT render description; if properties overflow, DROP them rather than wrap (keeps every card equal height). Row vertical rhythm and card padding on the 4px grid (4/8/12/16). Equal-height cards are what make a column scannable at a glance.
recommendations.where: Plane issue-card and list-row components (set fixed min/max-height, white-space:nowrap + text-overflow:ellipsis on title, hide description node, cap visible properties); pair with the display-property toggles below

recommendations.title: Configure board grouping + sub-group swimlanes + sticky counted headers to expose the 12-state workflow
recommendations.detail: Use Plane's grouping to make COLUMNS = the 12 agent-workflow states (in pipeline order), and enable sub-grouping (swimlanes) by Epic or by Actor/Agent to add the second observability axis. Make group headers sticky on scroll with a per-group COUNT badge (WIP-per-state is a free, high-value signal for a control plane). Enable 'show empty groups' so a state with zero items is still visibly present (absence is information). Order groups manually to match the canonical state sequence rather than alphabetical. This turns the board itself into a live state-machine visualization.
recommendations.where: Plane board view → Display options: Group by = State (ordered to the 12-state sequence), Sub-group/swimlane = Epic or Agent, Show empty groups = on, sticky group headers with count

recommendations.title: Curate visible card properties to the minimum legible set; hide the rest
recommendations.detail: Linear's legibility comes from showing FEW properties per card, not all. For the SDLC board, show on-card: State icon, Priority glyph, ID, Title, Assignee/Agent, and ONE of {Epic, Cycle} — hide created/updated dates, estimates, links, sub-issue counts from the card face (still available in the detail panel / on Space-preview). Fewer fields = lower height, faster scan, and a cleaner screenshot for machine consumption. Use Plane's display-property toggles to enforce this per view.
recommendations.where: Plane board/list view → Display options → Display properties (toggle off non-essential properties: created/updated date, estimate, link count, sub-issue count; keep state, priority, ID, assignee, cycle/epic)

recommendations.title: Apply Inter (body) + Inter Display (headings) + a mono for IDs/code
recommendations.detail: Type system matching Linear+Notion: body & UI = Inter Variable (or system-ui fallback) at 13px/20px line-height for rows, 14px for primary content; section/group headers = Inter Display (or Inter 600) at 13-14px uppercase-tracked or 15px medium; issue IDs, hashes, commit refs, and any machine identifier = a monospace (Berkeley Mono → fallback ui-monospace/'SF Mono') at 12px #8A8F98 — mono on IDs is both a Linear signature AND a machine-readability cue (identifiers are visually flagged as literal/copyable). Weights limited to 400/500/600. Tracking: -0.01em on titles for the crisp Linear feel; group headers can use +0.02em.
recommendations.where: Plane theme typography — set --font-sans = Inter, --font-mono = Berkeley Mono/ui-monospace; apply mono class to issue-ID spans; cap font-weights and set the px/line-height scale in the override stylesheet

recommendations.title: Use ONE indigo accent, sparingly — focus rings, primary actions, active selection only
recommendations.detail: Reserve a single accent (Plane --color-primary-*) for: focus rings, the primary 'New issue' button, active nav item, selected-row left-border, and drag-drop target highlight — nothing decorative. Light-theme accent = oklch(0.58 0.16 270) ≈ #5B5BD6 / indigo (a deeper, more legible cousin of Linear's #8FA6FF, which is dark-mode-tuned). It must hit 3:1 vs white for the focus ring (oklch 0.58 indigo ≈ 4.5:1, passes). Selected-row treatment = 2px left border in accent + #F6F5F4 wash, NOT a saturated fill. Keeping chrome-color scarce is Linear's explicit stated technique for neutrality.
recommendations.where: Plane --color-primary-100..900 ramp override; apply to button-primary, focus-visible outline, active-nav, selected-row::before left border, dnd-over highlight

recommendations.title: Add a barely-there hover/selection wash + 1px hairline borders instead of shadows
recommendations.detail: Interaction affordances stay grayscale and soft (Notion-refined): row/card hover = background #F6F5F4 or rgba(0,0,0,0.035) with NO border change and NO lift; selected = rgba(91,91,214,0.06) indigo wash + accent left-border; column/card separation = 1px hairline #E8E8E6, NOT box-shadow (Linear's board avoids elevation shadows on cards to stay flat/calm — reserve a single small shadow oklch-black/8% only for overlays/popovers/the Space-preview). Transitions: 120-150ms ease on hover bg only. Flat + hairline + faint-wash = calm; shadows + lifts = noisy.
recommendations.where: Plane card/row :hover and [aria-selected] states, column dividers, popover/overlay elevation — override background-color/border, remove card box-shadow, scope one subtle shadow token to overlays only

recommendations.title: Standardize spacing on a 4px grid with Linear-tight gutters and Notion-roomy radii
recommendations.detail: Spacing scale = 4/8/12/16/24/32/48 (4px base, shared by Linear-discipline and Notion). Concrete: column gutter 12px, column inner padding 8px, card gap (vertical) 6px, card inner padding 10-12px, row inner padding 8px 12px, chip gap 6px, icon-to-text gap 6px. Radii (the Notion-warm dial): chips/pills 5px, cards 8px, buttons 6px, panels/popovers 10px, avatars full. Keep gutters tight (Linear density) but radii slightly generous (Notion softness) — this exact combination is what produces 'crisp + dense yet warm and calm' rather than either austere-cold or bubbly-soft.
recommendations.where: Plane theme spacing/radius tokens — set --radius-* (chip 5px, card 8px, button 6px, popover 10px) and apply the 4px-grid padding/gap values to column, card, row, and chip components in the override stylesheet

recommendations.title: Make every property an independently-addressable, labeled inline-edit target (keyboard-first + observable)
recommendations.detail: Ensure each on-card property (state, priority, assignee, label, cycle) renders as a discrete element with a stable data attribute (data-property="state" data-value="in_review") and an accessible label, so it is: (a) click-to-edit in place for humans, (b) keyboard-focusable in tab order, (c) uniquely selectable by automation/screenshot-diff/observability tooling. This is the structural counterpart to Linear's inline-edit speed — it makes the board operable by humans at API speed and legible to machines field-by-field. Pair with Plane's existing inline-edit popovers; the addition is the stable data-attributes/labels for machine readability.
recommendations.where: Plane card/row property elements — add data-property / data-value attributes and aria-labels keyed to the state machine; document the attribute contract so agents/tests/observability can target fields deterministically

area: PM-SDLC board visual design — Notion-refined light theme restyled toward Linear (crisp/dense/calm), grounded in the live Plane board at audit/plane-ui/
summary: The current Plane board (ground truth: audit/plane-ui/screens/02-kanban-board.png, 01-issues-default.png, 03-ticket-detail.png; tokens at audit/plane-ui/design-tokens.json) is a competent but generic light theme — Inter Variable, oklch near-white body bg (0.9543), zero border-radius, no card elevation, narrow column cards with weak hierarchy and high-saturation editor label colors (#0fc27b, #266df0, #ff5b59) that clash with a calm aesthetic. The target — maximal human AND machine readability with a Linear-crisp / Notion-refined feel — is reachable through a disciplined token layer rather than structural change. The synthesis converges on five concrete moves: (1) a Linear-grade neutral text ramp with a hard contrast floor (primary text ~14.5:1, secondary ≥7:1, the lightest tertiary never below 4.5:1 for any real text — this fixes the readability/legibility goal directly and is the single highest-leverage change); (2) Notion's warm-neutral muted label palette (#787774 text on #F1F1EF-family tints) replacing Plane's saturated editor colors, so status/label chrome reads as quiet metadata not stoplights; (3) a strict 4px spacing base with Linear-tight card internals (8–12px padding, 8px inter-card gap) for density without crowding; (4) subtle structure — 1px hairline borders at ~#E6E6E3 / oklch(~0.92), 6–8px radius, near-invisible single-layer shadow only on hover, never resting; (5) machine-readability instrumentation baked into the same token pass — semantic data-* attributes, ARIA roles, stable status tokens, and contrast-verified state colors so the board is equally parseable by humans, screen readers, and agents. Typography moves to a Linear-style metric set: 13px/1.5 card titles at weight 510, 12px/1.4 uppercase-ish meta labels with negative tracking, 11–12px IDs in a mono face for scannability. Everything below is tagged by lever (css-theme / custom-property / content-convention / plane-setting / workflow-config) and carries specific px / hex / oklch / contrast values.
key_findings: GROUND TRUTH — current state: Plane board uses Inter Variable, body bg oklch(0.9543 0.001 230.67) (~#F1F1F2 cool gray), body text oklch(0.2378 0.0029 230.83) (~#2C2E33), radius 0px, shadow none, border same as text color (too heavy). Editor label colors are high-saturation (green #0fc27b, blue #266df0, peach #ff5b59, purple #9162f9) — these are the loudest thing on a board that should feel calm. Cards have no elevation, no radius, tight cramped text. This is the baseline to move FROM.

LINEAR'S CALM/CRISP FORMULA (primary source: linear.app/now/how-we-redesigned-the-linear-ui): color RESTRAINT (cut brand-blue influence on the neutral ramp for 'neutral and timeless'), LCH/oklch color space so equal-lightness colors read equally light, and deliberately DARKER text in light mode for contrast. Density comes from obsessive vertical+horizontal alignment of labels/icons/buttons, not from cramming. Inter Display for headings, regular Inter for body.

LINEAR LIGHT TOKENS (design.hagicode.com): surfaces #FFFFFF / #F7F8F8 / #F3F4F5; light border #D0D6E0 plus a tighter hairline near #E5E7EB; tertiary text #8A8F98, quaternary #62666D; brand indigo #5E6AD2. Type scale uses weight 510 (not 500) for UI emphasis, negative letter-spacing throughout (Caption 14px/-0.182px, Meta 13px/-0.13px, Label 12px), line-heights 1.4–1.6. Radius ladder 2/4/6/8/12px. Spacing 4/8/12/16/20/24/32. Elevation is layered and mostly a 1px ring, not a drop shadow.

NOTION IN-APP LIGHT COLORS (matthiasfrank.de, authoritative for the actual app not the marketing site): default text #373530 (warm near-black, NOT pure #000), gray text #787774, muted label text set {green #548164, blue #487CA5, purple #8A67AB, red #C4554D, orange #CC782F} — all DESATURATED vs Plane's editor colors. Highlight backgrounds are pale warm tints: gray #F1F1EF, green #EEF3ED, blue #E9F3F7, purple #F6F3F8, red #FAECEC. This text-on-tint pairing is the 'refined document' label look.

NOTION STRUCTURE (designmd.cc/benchmarks/notion): strict 4px spacing base; radius 8px and 12px on cards; multi-layer but extremely soft shadows (rgba(25,25,25,0.027) 0 8px 12px + 0 2px 6px) — barely-there elevation; muted secondary text #615D59 passes AA, subtle #A39E98 FAILS AA so it's used only for disabled/placeholder; border #DFDCD9 warm hairline; functional blue #097FE8 reserved strictly for primary actions. Hover lifts cards translateY(-2px) with a slightly stronger shadow.

CONTRAST FLOORS (WCAG 2.1/2.2): 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large (≥18px or ≥14px bold) and for UI component boundaries/focus indicators. Concrete pass on #FFFFFF: #373530 ≈ 11.8:1, #5C5E63 (Plane gray) ≈ 6.0:1, #787774 (Notion gray) ≈ 4.7:1 (passes, barely — do NOT go lighter for real text). Linear's #8A8F98 ≈ 3.2:1 — large-text/icon only, never body. This is the hard line for the 'maximal legibility' goal.

MACHINE READABILITY is a parallel requirement the visual layer must not break: status/priority must carry a non-color signal (icon shape + text label, never color alone — WCAG 1.4.1), every card needs a stable semantic ID token and ARIA role=listitem within role=list columns, and status colors should be exposed as named CSS custom properties (--status-backlog etc.) so both the DOM and downstream agents read one canonical token rather than inline hex. Transparency/observability favors persistent visible metadata (assignee, state, updated-at) over hover-only reveals.

DENSITY WITHOUT CRAMPING — the synthesis target: card internal padding 8–12px, 8px gap between cards, 6px radius, 1px hairline border at rest (oklch ~0.92 / ~#E6E6E3) instead of shadow, single soft shadow ONLY on hover. Column header 12px uppercase-tracked label + count pill. Title 13px/1.5 weight 510, meta row 11–12px. This matches Linear's information density while keeping Notion's air.

recommendations.title: Replace the body/surface palette with a warm-neutral Linear/Notion light ramp
recommendations.detail: Move off the cool oklch(0.9543 0.001 230.67) body bg. Set app canvas to a faint warm gray and cards/columns to white so cards visually lift without shadow. Concrete: --bg-canvas: oklch(0.984 0.002 95) (~#F7F7F5, warm like Notion #F6F5F4); --bg-surface: #FFFFFF; --bg-subtle: oklch(0.972 0.002 95) (~#F1F1EF) for column lanes and hover rows. Keep saturation near-zero with a slightly warm hue (~95) — this is what makes it read 'document' not 'dashboard'. Avoid pure-cool grays (hue 230) which feel clinical.
recommendations.where: Plane theme CSS / :root custom properties (the --editor-colors-* and body tokens captured in audit/plane-ui/design-tokens.json)

recommendations.title: Define a contrast-verified neutral text ramp with a hard 4.5:1 floor
recommendations.detail: Three text levels, all verified on #FFFFFF: --text-primary: #373530 (Notion warm near-black, ~11.8:1) for titles/body; --text-secondary: #5C5E63 (~6.0:1) for meta/descriptions; --text-tertiary: #787774 (~4.7:1) for the lightest real text (timestamps, counts) — this is the floor, never go lighter for anything a human must read. Reserve #8A8F98 / #A39E98 (sub-4.5:1) strictly for disabled state, placeholder, and decorative icons. This single change is the highest-leverage move for the 'maximal legibility' mandate.
recommendations.where: Theme :root — new --text-primary/secondary/tertiary tokens replacing the single body color oklch(0.2378 ...)

recommendations.title: Desaturate status/label colors to Notion's muted text-on-tint pairs
recommendations.detail: Swap Plane's high-saturation editor colors (#0fc27b, #266df0, #ff5b59, #9162f9) for Notion's muted label set so chrome reads as quiet metadata. Pair each label as desaturated text on a pale tint: green #548164 on #EEF3ED, blue #487CA5 on #E9F3F7, red/blocked #C4554D on #FAECEC, purple #8A67AB on #F6F3F8, orange #CC782F on #F8ECDF, neutral #787774 on #F1F1EF. Verify each text/tint pair ≥4.5:1. Reserve ONE saturated accent — a single brand color, e.g. Linear indigo #5E6AD2 or Notion blue #097FE8 — exclusively for primary buttons, active nav, and focus rings. Restraint is the aesthetic.
recommendations.where: --editor-colors-*-text and --editor-colors-*-background vars in design-tokens.json; Plane label/state color definitions

recommendations.title: Adopt a Linear-metric type scale with negative tracking and weight 510
recommendations.detail: Keep Inter Variable (already in use), add Inter Display for the one or two largest headings. Scale: card title 13px / line-height 1.5 / weight 510 / letter-spacing -0.13px; meta + secondary 12px / 1.4 / weight 510 / -0.13px; column header label 12px / weight 510 / letter-spacing +0.04em uppercase; ticket-detail H1 24px / 1.33 / weight 510 / -0.288px; body 15px / 1.6. Use a mono face (e.g. Berkeley Mono or ui-monospace) at 11–12px for issue IDs like AGCP-E8-24 so they scan as machine tokens. Weight 510 (not 500) is Linear's signature crisp-but-not-bold UI weight via variable-font axis.
recommendations.where: Typography tokens; per-component font-size/weight/letter-spacing on card title, meta row, column header, issue-ID badge

recommendations.title: Add subtle structure: hairline borders, 6px radius, hover-only elevation
recommendations.detail: Cards: 1px solid border at --border-hairline: oklch(0.92 0.002 95) (~#E6E6E3, warm) — Plane currently uses border = text color which is far too heavy. border-radius: 6px (Linear) on cards, 8px on panels. At REST: no shadow, just the hairline (Linear's flat+ring look). On HOVER: --bg-subtle row tint + a single soft shadow 0 1px 3px rgba(20,20,20,0.04) + 0 4px 8px rgba(20,20,20,0.04), optional translateY(-1px). Column lanes get no border, only a faint #F7F7F5 fill. Dividers between list rows: 1px #EFEFED, full-bleed. This delivers 'subtle borders/dividers, refined-document feel' without visual noise.
recommendations.where: Card, column-lane, list-row, and divider component styles; replaces body radius 0px / shadow none / border=text-color

recommendations.title: Tighten spacing to a strict 4px base for Linear density without crowding
recommendations.detail: Spacing scale 4/8/12/16/20/24/32. Card internal padding 10–12px; gap between cards in a lane 8px; lane horizontal padding 8px, lane gap 12px; meta-row item gap 6px; icon-to-label gap 6px with baseline alignment. Board top toolbar height 40px, list-row height 36–40px. The discipline is Linear's: density comes from consistent small gaps and pixel-aligned icons/labels, not from removing whitespace — keep 8px+ around any text so it never touches a border.
recommendations.where: Spacing tokens + card/lane/row padding and gap declarations

recommendations.title: Make status/priority machine- and screen-reader-readable, not color-only
recommendations.detail: Never encode state in color alone (WCAG 1.4.1). Each status chip = shaped icon + text label + tint (e.g. Backlog = hollow circle, In Progress = half-filled, Done = filled check). Expose canonical tokens as CSS custom properties (--status-backlog, --status-in-progress, --status-done, --priority-urgent...) so the DOM carries one source of truth instead of inline hex. Add data-status / data-priority / data-issue-id attributes and ARIA (column = role=list with aria-label, card = role=listitem, draggable announced). Keep assignee, state, and updated-at as PERSISTENT visible metadata (not hover-only) to serve the transparency/observability goal — agents and humans both read the resting DOM.
recommendations.where: Card component markup + status/priority token map; Plane custom-field / template conventions

recommendations.title: Standardize focus, selection, and interactive affordances around one accent
recommendations.detail: Focus ring: 2px solid accent at 35% alpha + 1px inset, e.g. box-shadow 0 0 0 2px rgba(94,106,210,0.35) (≥3:1 against surface per WCAG 2.2 focus-appearance). Selected card: 1px accent border + faint accent tint bg (rgba(94,106,210,0.06)). Hover affordances: cursor + #F7F7F5 tint + reveal secondary actions at 60→100% opacity on hover but keep them in the DOM at rest for accessibility. All transitions 0.15–0.2s ease-out, transform/opacity/background only (no layout thrash). One accent color does focus, selection, primary action, and active nav — nothing else competes.
recommendations.where: Focus/selection/hover state styles across card, list-row, button, nav-item; single --accent custom property

recommendations.title: Configure board columns and field display for legibility and observability
recommendations.detail: Order workflow columns left-to-right by SDLC flow (Backlog -> Triaged -> Spec -> Spec-Verified -> Build -> Review -> Done) with a count pill per column header (12px tertiary text). Surface on every card by default: issue-ID (mono), title (2-line clamp, not 1 — current board truncates too aggressively), status icon, priority, assignee avatar, and an updated-at relative timestamp — this is the observability/transparency payload. Limit visible labels per card to ~3 with a +N overflow chip to preserve calm. Use Plane's display/grouping settings to group by state and sort by priority then updated.
recommendations.where: Plane board view settings (column order, group-by, card field display, label limits) — the Display menu visible in 02-kanban-board.png

area: Light-theme design system for a Linear/Notion-style PM-SDLC board (neutral ramps, 4.5:1+ text contrast, border-led elevation, one accent, semantic status colors) — with concrete oklch/hex tokens
summary: The brief is achievable with a luminance-first OKLCH system layered onto a warm-neutral gray ramp, exactly the direction Linear's own 2025/26 refresh took (cool blue-gray to warmer, less-saturated gray; "hierarchy through restraint"; softened borders over heavy dividers) and that Notion executes with near-imperceptible multi-layer shadows on a white base. For this board the hard constraint is NOT aesthetics but DENSITY + DISTINGUISHABILITY: 12 workflow states + 22 labels must each be unambiguous to a human at 11-12px pill size AND machine-parseable. The winning pattern is the Notion label model — a low-chroma background TINT paired with a high-L-contrast same-hue text/foreground color — generated systematically in OKLCH so every status pill hits the same lightness math and therefore the same contrast ratio regardless of hue. Surfaces should be a 3-step warm-gray ramp (canvas slightly off-white, cards pure white, sunken/hover one notch darker), elevation carried by 1px borders + one whisper shadow rather than Material-style drop shadows, and exactly ONE accent (indigo/violet, Linear's signature) reserved for primary action + active/focus state only. Body text on white must sit at L~25-38% (>=7:1, comfortably past 4.5:1); secondary/muted text must not drop below L~50% on white or it fails AA. For observability/machine-readability, every state color should be emitted as a named CSS custom property AND carry a non-color encoding (icon shape + text label + data-state attribute) so the board never relies on color alone — this is both a WCAG 1.4.1 requirement and what makes the board diff-able/scrapable by agents. APCA (not just WCAG 2.x) should be the gate for the small dense text because WCAG 2.x mis-rates thin UI type.
key_findings: DIRECTION IS VALIDATED BY THE SOURCE: Linear's 2025/26 refresh explicitly moved from a 'cool, blue-ish' gray toward a 'warmer gray that still feels crisp, but less saturated', and darkened text/neutral icons in light mode to raise contrast. Its two stated principles map 1:1 to this brief: (1) 'Hierarchy through restraint' — not every element carries equal weight; secondary nav recedes, primary work area stays focal; (2) 'Subtle structure' — borders/separators with SOFTENED contrast and rounded edges, not harsh dividers. Concrete moves: dimmer sidebar, muted inactive text, smaller icon scales, removed colored icon backgrounds, icon-only compact tab pills.

OKLCH IS THE RIGHT ENGINE FOR A MULTI-STATUS BOARD: because OKLCH lightness is perceptually uniform, if you hold the L value constant across hues the contrast ratio is also held constant. So all 12 state pills can share one L-pair (e.g. tint at L~95%, text at L~45%) and every single one lands at the same AA-passing ratio regardless of whether it's red, green, or indigo. Validated example ramp from the source: text at shade-700/800 (L 25-38%) on background shade-200 (L 89%) measured 7.2:1 — well past 4.5:1. Neutrals use chroma 0.01; semantic accents peak chroma ~0.20-0.27 at the 500 mid-step and taper to ~0.02 at the 100/900 extremes to avoid oversaturated near-white/near-black.

NEUTRAL RAMP (light theme, warm gray, hue ~95-100 for warmth, chroma 0.004-0.008): canvas/app-bg oklch(98.5% 0.004 95) #FBFBFA; surface/card oklch(100% 0 0) #FFFFFF; subtle/sunken oklch(97% 0.005 95) #F6F6F4; hover oklch(96.5% 0.006 95) #F1F1EF; border-subtle oklch(92% 0.006 95) #E8E8E4; border-default oklch(88% 0.007 95) #DDDDD8; border-strong oklch(82% 0.008 95) #C9C9C2; text-tertiary/placeholder oklch(62% 0.01 95) #9A9A92 (use only >=16px or non-essential); text-secondary oklch(50% 0.012 95) #76766E (~5:1 on white, passes AA body); text-primary oklch(32% 0.012 95) #3A3A36 (~10:1); text-strong/heading oklch(24% 0.012 95) #28282452. These mirror Notion's actual neutrals (its gray label is bg #F1F1EF / text #787774).

ELEVATION = BORDER-FIRST, SHADOW AS A WHISPER (Notion's real model): cards lift with a 1px border + a multi-layer near-zero-opacity shadow, e.g. resting card box-shadow: 0 1px 2px rgba(15,15,15,0.04); raised menu/popover: 0 1px 2px rgba(15,15,15,0.05), 0 4px 12px rgba(15,15,15,0.08); dragged card: 0 8px 24px rgba(15,15,15,0.12). Notion's documented shadows are 'almost imperceptible... multiple low-opacity layers to mimic real-world lighting.' Radius scale 6px (pills/inputs) / 8px (cards) / 12px (panels/modals) — Notion uses 8 and 12. Keep all shadow tints neutral-cool (rgba of near-black 15,15,15), never the accent.

ONE RESTRAINED ACCENT: use a single indigo/violet (Linear's signature family) ONLY for primary buttons, active nav item, focus ring, selected state, and links. accent-default oklch(54% 0.20 274) ~ #5B5BD6; accent-hover oklch(49% 0.21 274) ~ #4F4FCC; accent-fg-on-white text/links oklch(50% 0.19 274) (>=4.5:1 on white); accent-subtle-bg (selected row tint) oklch(96% 0.03 274) ~ #EEEEFB; focus-ring oklch(62% 0.18 274) at 2px + 2px offset. Do NOT use the accent for status — status owns the semantic hues, the accent owns interaction. This separation is what keeps a dense board calm.

SEMANTIC STATUS PALETTE built the Notion way (low-chroma tint bg + same-hue darker text), one row per state, ALL sharing tint L~95% / text L~45% so contrast is uniform and AA-safe. Map the 12 SDLC states to hues: Backlog=gray oklch(95% 0.004 95)/text oklch(46% 0.01 95); Agent-Triaged=slate-blue; Spec-Compiling=amber oklch(95% 0.04 75)/oklch(52% 0.13 70); Spec-Verified=teal; Plan-Approved=blue oklch(95% 0.03 240)/oklch(48% 0.13 245) (~Notion blue #E9F3F7/#487CA5); Agent-Executing=indigo (but DESATURATE vs brand accent to avoid clash, or use violet); In-Verification=cyan; Human-Review=purple oklch(95% 0.03 300)/oklch(50% 0.15 300) (~Notion purple #F6F3F8/#8A67AB); Done=green oklch(95% 0.03 150)/oklch(50% 0.13 155) (~Notion green #EEF3ED/#548164); HANDOFF=orange oklch(95% 0.04 60)/oklch(52% 0.14 55) (~Notion orange #F8ECDF/#CC782F); Blocked=red oklch(95% 0.03 25)/oklch(52% 0.17 27) (~Notion red #FAECEC/#C4554D); Failed=deep red/maroon (darker than Blocked so the two read as distinct severities, not the same alarm).

NEVER COLOR-ALONE (machine + accessibility requirement): with 12 states, hue alone is not enough for colorblind users OR for reliable agent scraping. Each state pill must carry THREE redundant encodings: (a) the color tint, (b) a unique leading icon/glyph (e.g. dot=backlog, half-circle=in-progress, check=done, slash=blocked, triangle=failed — Linear's status-icon vocabulary), and (c) the literal text label. In markup, emit a stable data-state="spec-verified" attribute and aria-label so the DOM is unambiguous to agents and screen readers independent of pixels. This satisfies WCAG 1.4.1 (use of color) and is what makes the board observable/diff-able by the autonomous loop.

DENSITY + TYPE (Linear-crisp): use an 8px spacing grid but allow 4px half-steps for dense rows. Board column gutter 8px; card padding 8-12px; card vertical rhythm 4px between meta rows; row height for list view 32-36px. Type scale (system UI / Inter-class, -0.01em tracking on headings): 11px meta/labels (line-height 16px, weight 500, often uppercase tracking +0.04em for state pills), 13px body/card title (LH 18-20px, weight 450-500), 14px section headers, 16px page title, 20-24px view title. Linear's refresh used SMALLER icon scales and compact tab pills — bias toward 13px as the workhorse body size, not 16px, for board density. Keep weight contrast (450 body vs 600 heading) rather than size jumps to stay calm.

CONTRAST GATING: gate small/dense UI text with APCA (Lc), not WCAG 2.x, because WCAG 2.x systematically mis-scores thin sans-serif at small sizes. Targets: body/labels Lc >=75 (roughly WCAG 4.5:1+), large/heading Lc >=60, non-essential/disabled Lc ~45. On pure white #FFF, text-secondary must not go lighter than L~52% or it slips under 4.5:1 — that's the single most common light-theme failure. Validate every status text-on-tint pair AND every text-on-surface pair; because the OKLCH L values are held constant the whole matrix passes once one pair does.

recommendations.title: Ship a luminance-first OKLCH token file with a 3-surface warm-neutral ramp and held-constant L steps
recommendations.detail: Author tokens.css with the neutral ramp above (canvas #FBFBFA, surface #FFFFFF, subtle #F6F6F4, hover #F1F1EF; borders #E8E8E4/#DDDDD8/#C9C9C2; text #28282452/#3A3A36/#76766E/#9A9A92). Express every value as oklch() with hue ~95 and chroma 0.004-0.012 for warmth, then provide a hex fallback. Hold text L at 32% (primary) and 50% (secondary) so contrast on white is locked at ~10:1 and ~5:1. This is the single source of truth both the human CSS and any agent reading computed styles consume.
recommendations.where: New tokens.css / :root custom properties consumed by the board UI (no UI layer exists yet in plane-integration — this is greenfield for the board skin).

recommendations.title: Define the 12-state semantic palette as one OKLCH matrix with constant tint-L (95%) and text-L (45%)
recommendations.detail: Generate the 12 state colors by varying ONLY hue across the Notion-derived hue wheel (gray 95, amber 75, teal 180, blue 240, violet 285, indigo 274-desaturated, cyan 200, purple 300, green 150, orange 60, red 25, maroon 20). Hold tint at oklch(95% C H) and foreground at oklch(45% C H). Because L is constant, every pill lands at the same AA-passing ratio. Make Blocked (red, L45) and Failed (maroon, L35) deliberately different lightness so the two failure states never read as identical. Emit each as --state-<name>-bg and --state-<name>-fg.
recommendations.where: tokens.css state block; consumed by the 12 workflow states defined in README §'Control vocabulary' (Backlog…Done + HANDOFF/Blocked/Failed).
